title: Gooseberry and elderflower cake
subtitle: A light sponge with sharp gooseberries and fragrant elderflower
excerpt: Gooseberries and elderflower are one of the natural pairings of early British summer. The gooseberries are folded into the batter dusted with flour to help them stay suspended, and elderflower cordial replaces some of the liquid to carry the fragrance through every slice. The cake bakes in about 50 minutes and keeps well for two days.
sourceNotes: Gooseberries and elderflower ripen simultaneously in early British summer and have been paired in preserves, cordials and desserts since at least the nineteenth century. The combination appears in Jane Grigson's 'Fruit Book' (1982) and later in recipes by Nigel Slater and Diana Henry. The use of elderflower cordial as a baking flavour became widespread in British cookbooks from the 1990s onwards.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze plain (no dusting) for up to 2 months. Thaw at room temperature for 2 hours.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake keeps for up to 2 days at room temperature, wrapped. The elderflower flavour becomes more pronounced on the second day.

glossaryTerms.definition: Tossing fruit pieces in a tablespoon of flour before folding them into cake batter. The flour creates a light coating that increases friction between the fruit and the batter, helping the pieces stay suspended rather than sinking to the base during baking. The technique is particularly useful with heavy, moisture-rich fruit like gooseberries, blueberries or cherries in a lighter sponge batter.

content.text: on the gooseberries before adding them to the batter. Without it, their weight and moisture content will carry them straight to the base of the tin and you will end up with a fruit-free top and a wet bottom layer.

Use an undiluted elderflower cordial rather than a diluted elderflower drink. The cordial is concentrated and contributes flavour without adding too much liquid. Add it with the milk; the two together should feel like the standard amount of liquid for a sponge of this size.

items.prepNote: plus 1 tbsp for dusting the fruit

content.text: Heat the oven to 170 degrees C fan. Grease and line a deep 20 cm round cake tin. Toss the 250 g topped-and-tailed gooseberries with 1 tbsp of the measured flour and set aside.

Beat the 175 g butter and 200 g caster sugar together until pale and fluffy, about 3 minutes. Beat in the 3 eggs one at a time. Combine the remaining flour with the 1.5 tsp baking powder and fold in. Stir in the 3 tbsp elderflower cordial and 2 tbsp milk to loosen to a dropping consistency.

Fold the flour-dusted gooseberries into the batter with a large spoon. Pour into the prepared tin and level the surface. Bake for 48 to 55 minutes until the top is golden brown, the edges are pulling from the tin, and a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean. Cool in the tin for 15 minutes, then on a rack. Dust with icing sugar before serving.

items.problem: All the gooseberries sank to the base
items.solution: The batter was too loose or the fruit was not floured. The batter should be a thick dropping consistency, not pourable. If it seems thin, add 1 to 2 tbsp extra flour before folding in the fruit.
The cordial used was too dilute or the brand was mild. Use undiluted cordial rather than a ready-to-drink elderflower drink, and increase to 4 tbsp if the brand is particularly subtle.

items.problem: Wet, sticky patches in the crumb
items.solution: The cake was underbaked. Gooseberries release juice as they cook, which can slow the set in the centre. A skewer may come out with fruit juice but should not carry wet batter. If needed, cover with foil and bake for a further 8 to 10 minutes.
